figures.caption: Figure 5: 2D system. (a) Potential energy function; (b) χ-function; (c) Effective potential; (d) Effective diffusion; (e) Eigenvalues.
Figure 6: 2D system. (a) MFPT in full space; (b) MFPT in latent space; (c) Committor in full space; (d) Committor in latent space; (f) TPT rates of full and effective dynamics.

Figure 1: Illustration of the transformation from eigen- to χ-functions. Left: Dominant eigenfunctions φi(x). Middle: Eigenfunction components plotted in the plane φ1 ≡ 1, with an enclosing simplex indicated. Right: The transformation that maps this simplex to the unit simplex sends φ to χ, which then lies on the unit simplex.
Figure 7: 3D system. (a) Potential energy function; (b,c,d) Three χ-functions; (e) Effective potential on ∆̄ 2 ; (f-h) Diffusion tensor entries on ∆̄ 2 .

Figure 2: Comparison of the three components of χ : R3 → R3 obtained from either ISOKANN (blue) or direct discretization through the SqRA (red) for the potential from Section 5.3. Each plot displays one component of χ evaluated at one of 10 000 training samples, where the samples displayed on the horizontal axis were sorted by the respective χ value (different for each subfigure) obtained from SqRA to allow for a visual comparison. The random fluctuations that are seemingly visible for the χ-functions computed via ISOKANN are the values on samples rather far away from all other samples; the gradients of the χ-functions are still smooth functions and agree well for SqRA and ISOKANN. The difference in scale of the second component results from details of the ISA algorithm and do not have a significant impact on the effective dynamics.
Figure 3: 1D system. (a) Potential energy function; (b) χ-function; (c) Effective potential; (d) Effective diffusion; (e) Eigenvalues.
Figure 8: 3D system. (a) Eigenvalues of L and L̃; (b) Committor in full space; (c) Committor q projected on ∆̄ 2 ; (d) Effective committor q̃; (e) TPT rates of full and effective dynamics.
